questions.question: What is the main goal of rough cost modeling in a system design interview?
options: To identify which design choice becomes expensive first and compare alternatives directionally.
To reproduce exact cloud bills down to the cent.
To avoid discussing performance trade-offs.
To prove that the cheapest design is always best.
questions.explanation: Interview cost modeling is about decision support, not accounting perfection.

questions.question: Why is it useful to break cost into a few major buckets?
options: Because dominant spend drivers become visible and easier to reason about.
Because it guarantees the architecture is correct.
Because it removes the need for workload estimates.
Because all cost buckets scale linearly in the same way.
questions.explanation: A bucketed model surfaces where optimization is most valuable.

questions.question: Which bucket most often dominates internet-facing high-throughput systems?
options: Egress or bandwidth-related transfer costs
The number of architecture diagrams
The cost of code review tools
The count of API methods
questions.explanation: For byte-heavy systems, transfer cost can dominate monthly spend.

questions.question: What is the risk of false precision in cost discussions?
options: It consumes time without improving the architectural decision much.
It makes the storage layer strongly consistent.
It reduces egress cost automatically.
It removes the need for sensitivity analysis.
questions.explanation: Overly detailed pricing can distract from the actual design trade-off.

questions.question: Which follow-up best demonstrates senior-level cost reasoning?
options: Pointing out which assumption swings the total most and how you would validate it.
Memorizing one provider's exact pricing page.
Ignoring cost until the final minute.
Always rejecting managed services as too expensive.
questions.explanation: Sensitivity analysis is more useful than memorized price trivia.

questions.question: Which observability view best supports ongoing cost discipline?
options: Spend by service and bucket, tied to real workload metrics
Number of team chat messages
Count of repositories in the organization
Average code comment length
questions.explanation: You need cost signals connected to usage drivers.

questions.question: What trade-off often justifies paying more for a managed system?
options: Higher direct spend in exchange for lower operational complexity and risk.
Higher latency in exchange for lower consistency.
Lower egress in exchange for higher retention.
More retries in exchange for cheaper storage.
questions.explanation: The right answer is often about total operational cost, not only infra line items.

questions.question: When should a candidate compare two cost variants instead of quoting one total?
options: When the architecture decision is between meaningfully different operating models or scaling levers.
Only when the interviewer asks for a spreadsheet.
Never, because one number is always clearer.
Only when storage is the dominant line item.
questions.explanation: Variants reveal trade-offs that a single total often hides.
